name: database-architect
description: Expert database architect specializing in data layer design from
capabilities: Recommend database technologies based on requirements and trade-offs
Design normalized schemas with proper relationships and constraints
Create indexing strategies for query optimization
Plan zero-downtime migration strategies with rollback procedures
Design caching architectures and replication patterns
Model multi-tenant and sharded database architectures
author: sickn33
securitySummary: Static analysis scanned 0 files with risk score 0/100. Evaluation confirms this is a prompt-only skill with no executable code. The skill provides database architecture guidance through instructional text only. No dangerous patterns, network access, or code execution vectors detected. Safe for publication.
valueStatement: Choosing the wrong database or poor schema design leads to costly rework and performance problems. This skill provides expert guidance on database technology selection, data modeling, and architecture design to build scalable data layers from the start.
limitations: Provides design recommendations only, does not execute database changes
Does not perform actual query tuning on existing systems
Cannot modify database schemas or run migrations without explicit request
Focuses on data layer design, not application-level feature design

useCases.targetUser: Startup CTO building a multi-tenant e-commerce platform from scratch
useCases.title: Greenfield Platform Design
useCases.description: Design a complete database architecture for a new SaaS platform including technology selection, schema design, and scaling strategy.

useCases.targetUser: Engineering lead re-architecting a legacy monolith into microservices
useCases.title: Database Migration Planning
useCases.description: Create a detailed migration plan to move from a monolithic MySQL database to a microservices architecture with polyglot persistence.

useCases.targetUser: Developer building real-time analytics requiring millions of writes per day
useCases.title: NoSQL Schema Design
useCases.description: Design document schemas and access patterns for a high-velocity analytics dashboard using MongoDB or DynamoDB.

promptTemplates.title: Basic Technology Selection
promptTemplates.prompt: I'm building a new application that needs to store user profiles, transactions, and activity logs. The app expects 10,000 daily active users initially. Help me choose the right database technology and explain the trade-offs.
promptTemplates.scenario: Early-stage startup selecting their first database

promptTemplates.title: Schema Design Request
promptTemplates.prompt: Design a database schema for a multi-tenant project management tool. Each tenant has users, projects, tasks, and comments. Show the tables, relationships, and key indexes needed.

promptTemplates.title: Migration Strategy Planning
promptTemplates.prompt: We need to migrate from a single MySQL instance to a sharded architecture supporting 100M+ records. Create a zero-downtime migration plan with phases, rollback procedures, and success criteria.

promptTemplates.title: Advanced CQRS Architecture
promptTemplates.prompt: Design a CQRS event-sourced architecture for an order management system. Include event store design, read model projections, snapshot strategies, and how to handle schema evolution over time.
promptTemplates.scenario: Complex domain requiring audit trails and temporal queries

bestPractices: Always understand access patterns and scale requirements before selecting database technology
Start normalized, then denormalize selectively based on measured query performance
Plan migrations with automated rollback procedures and test thoroughly in staging
antiPatterns: Choosing trendy databases without understanding operational complexity
Over-normalizing read-heavy workloads causing excessive JOIN operations
Skipping backup and rollback planning before production migrations

faq.question: What database should I choose for my startup?
faq.answer: Start with PostgreSQL for most applications. It handles relational data well, supports JSON for flexibility, and scales to millions of users. Switch to specialized databases only when you have specific needs like time-series data, graph relationships, or massive write throughput.

faq.question: How do I know when to shard my database?
faq.answer: Consider sharding when vertical scaling becomes cost-prohibitive, write throughput exceeds single-node capacity, or data volume impacts backup and maintenance windows. First optimize with read replicas, caching, and query optimization before sharding.

faq.question: Should I use an ORM or write raw SQL?
faq.answer: Use ORMs like Prisma or SQLAlchemy for CRUD operations and type safety. Write raw SQL for complex analytical queries, bulk operations, or when the ORM generates inefficient queries. Many teams use both: ORM for standard operations and raw SQL for performance-critical paths.

faq.question: How do I design for multi-tenancy?
faq.answer: Three approaches exist: shared schema with tenant_id column (simplest), separate schema per tenant (better isolation), or separate database per tenant (maximum isolation). Start with shared schema and tenant_id. Upgrade to separate schemas or databases for compliance requirements or high-value enterprise customers.

faq.question: What indexing strategy should I use?
faq.answer: Index columns used in WHERE clauses, JOIN conditions, and ORDER BY statements. Use composite indexes for multi-column queries with the most selective column first. Monitor slow query logs to identify missing indexes. Avoid over-indexing write-heavy tables as each index adds write overhead.

faq.question: How can I migrate with zero downtime?
faq.answer: Use the expand-contract pattern: first add new schema alongside old (expand), migrate data incrementally with dual writes, verify consistency, switch reads to new schema, then remove old schema (contract). Always have rollback procedures and test the full migration in staging.
